html:
XV

The Aquitania had crept up the river that morning with exasperating caution. It was the day before Christmas. The more impatient compared tedious experiences in customs; hoped goodwill toward men might have percolated through to the baggage inspectors; wondered if they could get Pullman space on the two-forty-five in case they missed the one-twenty.

Her furs muffled high about her throat, Helen Hudson ventured to the frosty rail of B-deck as they passed the Battery, and found the stinging breeze a bit strenuous. She had avoided three consecutive winters, and the fast trip from Nice to New York had not been free of discomfort.

A spluttery scrawl from Joyce accounted for her sudden decision to return. She had read the letter on a stone bench by the sea wall, a stone‚Äôs throw from the Casino pier, one week ago this morning‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ It had been that kind of a journey.

‚ÄúAll packed and waiting for the taxi,‚Äù Joyce had begun. ‚ÄúGoing back to Detroit. Why Detroit, I don‚Äôt know, unless because it seems to offer something like anchorage. I‚Äôll try to find some employment there‚ÅÝ‚Äîanything to occupy my mind‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ The past month has been a nightmare! Quite unendurable! Last night, Tom struck me a savage blow on the breast with his fist‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ deeply penitent afterwards‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ wailed his remorse like a little child‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ But I told him I‚Äôd had enough‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ I‚Äôm through!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ He left, early this morning, ashamed and glum. He thinks I‚Äôll be here when he comes home tonight, though I made it plain enough I was leaving‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ I wonder, darling, would it be asking too much of you to come back and be with me for a few weeks. I do need some good counsel, and I‚Äôm bitterly lonely‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ It‚Äôs a shame to drag you back to Detroit in midwinter‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut could you? I‚Äôve nobody but you! I need you desperately!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Cable me at the Statler‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ I‚Äôll be wild with joy if you say you‚Äôll come!‚Äù

It had taken only an hour to decide. Creasing the letter with puzzled fingers, she had risen and walked mechanically along the Promenade des Anglais for fully half a mile; had slowly retraced her steps, past the Negresso and the Ruhl, so deep in her problem she barely noticed the strollers recruited from a dozen nations. By the time she had reached the little park fronting the Jettee, Helen was ready to concede that Joyce had won her case.

She had called the roll of all the alternatives to a return trip. Why not cable Joyce to come over? But no‚ÅÝ‚ÄîJoyce had set her heart on finding some employment. She would find none along the Riviera. Joyce wanted to begin life again. She couldn‚Äôt do it lounging in Nice. Joyce was bitterly lonely. Well‚ÅÝ‚ÄîNice wouldn‚Äôt help that very much‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ After all‚ÅÝ‚Äîshe did owe Joyce some attention. She must go!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ It was almost as busy an afternoon as the one on which the had impulsively migrated from Bellagio.

Slowly the big ship warped into her slip; sleety, salty hawsers were wound upon huge rumbling spools somewhere deep in her vitals; the covered gangways were hauled up from the dock and opened to the swarm of restless home-comers. Almost everybody had located wide expectant eyes in the steam-breathing crowd that huddled at the openings of the wharf-shed.

Helen felt very much alone. She exchanged tentative goodbyes with the few friends she had made on board; doubtless she would be bumping into them again in the Customs.

Already a nervous little caucus had convened under the big H, halfway down the draughty warehouse where a pile of luggage was rapidly accumulating. Some sat on their trunks with the resigned despair of the shipwrecked on a desert island; some‚ÅÝ‚Äîless experienced‚ÅÝ‚Äîsquatted before their gaping bags contemplating last minute amendments to their declaration slips.

Young Mrs.¬ÝHudson had done a minimum of collecting in Europe, but it was amazing how many things one unwittingly came into possession of in the course of a mere three years of foreign travel. As she neared her letter, she met a pair of officials headed also in that direction and told them that she was hopeful of spending at least part of Christmas Day with relatives in Detroit. She led them to her belongings and indicated which pieces they were to put the stickers on. She could have brought in the Crown jewels, that morning.

To her delight, the three o‚Äôclock train was not crowded; but why should it be? It was Christmas Eve! Normal people were at home. The thought depressed her. For a little while, as the train roared through the tunnel, she missed a home as she had never before.

There was a pleasurable excitement, however, in this return to Detroit. She and Joyce had been very close. Whatever of constraint had come upon their comradeship, in that last trying winter when she had attempted so unsuccessfully to keep the girl from utterly ruining herself, Joyce‚Äôs urgent need of her drove all that into oblivion.

And there were many other friends in Detroit she would be happy to see again‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ The Byrnes‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Should she go out to see Mrs.¬ÝAshford? Why not‚ÅÝ‚Äîindeed? Mrs.¬ÝAshford had been good to her‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ It might be thought strange if Doctor Hudson‚Äôs widow returned to Detroit and failed to call at Brightwood‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Perhaps it would serve the purpose if she invited Mrs.¬ÝAshford down town for luncheon and a matin√©e‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ It wouldn‚Äôt be necessary to go out to the hospital‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Doctor Pyle would be glad to see her, of course; but she would call on the Pyles at their home‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Besides‚ÅÝ‚Äîit would be awkward meeting people one scarcely knew any more, and being expected to remember their names‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ And as for that glassblower, who meddled in people‚Äôs affairs and sent spies about to report on their movements, one would be almost sure to encounter him!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Why should he want to blow glass, anyway?‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Imagine!‚ÅÝ‚Äîa brain surgeon spending his time in such silly business!

The sky was the colour of wood-ashes. Big snowflakes flattened against the pane, crumbled, edged toward the lower corner of the sash. No matter where they struck and spattered, or how slowly they made their way, sooner or later, quickly or slowly, they eventually arrived in the corner and packed themselves down hard against the others‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Her thoughts were like that. Let them strike where they would‚ÅÝ‚Äîthey all contrived to bring up at one spot. She was impatient about it, tugged herself free of her reveries, returned to the magazine story and reread with utter disinterest the page that had failed to register‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Would it pay to take the coup√© out of the storage garage? She had not driven on icy streets for so long. Had she lost her nerve? Skidding was dangerous‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ She watched a snowflake creep across the pane‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Her eyes grew reminiscent, tender. She bit her lip. Her cheeks were flushed‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Then, vexed with herself, she shifted her position and took up the magazine with resolution.

Had Joyce been effectually cured of her drinking? Would she drift into her old habits, once she had renewed connections with her Detroit set?‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ And be brought in, at all hours, maudlin and stupid?‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ An unusually big snowflake went scudding across the glass, clawing for a hold, but unable to stand out against the rule for all snowflakes on this pane‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ For a brief instant, she was in his arms, and she felt his lips pressed hard against her own‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ She threw down the magazine and rang the bell for a table. A game of solitaire might divert her.

It was pleasant to be back in an American dining-car‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ And, all things considered, it would be better to invite Mrs.¬ÝAshford to take an afternoon off and come down town‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Our diners were so much nicer than the European ones where the passengers were organized into platoons and given so many minutes to devour each course‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ What a pity she had felt she had to sell her Brightwood stock. Would it make any difference in her relations with Mrs.¬ÝAshford?

It was bitterly cold that night as the long train clanked and creaked and screamed along the east bank of the Hudson, and, crossing the bridge at Albany, thrust its black muzzle into the face of a blinding blizzard; but the scurrying landscape, blurry with snow, did not seem so bleakly inhospitable as she had feared. In her berth, she raised on one elbow, drew aside the blind, and watched the sleet-trimmed trees and fences skimming by, half glad she was returning. She snuggled down with her furs against her cheek, and drifted off into an extended debate with somebody, whether she wasn‚Äôt under a sort of obligation to call at Brightwood, if only for a moment.

Joyce was at the gates, dancing gleefully, arms outstretched, as she sighted her beloved emerging from the concourse tunnel, pursued by two porters staggering under bags plastered with foreign labels. There were some murmured tendernesses, and a moment later they were careening around the circle, tire-chains rattling merrily. Bubbling with excitement, Joyce tried to tell half a dozen stories at once.

‚ÄúFirst thing I did, darling, when I arrived‚ÅÝ‚Äîweek ago‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI went out to see Nancy Ashford. Isn‚Äôt she the dearest thing? But you hardly knew her. Well‚ÅÝ‚Äîshe‚Äôs regular!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ I went out to Brightwood and told Nancy all about it; how I couldn‚Äôt stick it another minute and had had to leave him; and could I possibly find anything to do, just to keep from going crazy‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ And, what do you think? They had just lost a file-clerk, and would I like to do that for a week or two, to find out how I would react to the discipline of office hours; and, meanwhile, we could all be thinking and inquiring for something permanent‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ But, I don‚Äôt mind if nothing turns up for a while. Really‚ÅÝ‚Äîit isn‚Äôt dull work, at all; and I quite like it!‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, you‚Äôve begun it already?‚Äù

‚ÄúUmm-humm!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ That same afternoon!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Just took off my hat and went to it. Honestly‚ÅÝ‚Äîit‚Äôs a lark. Of course, I knew several of the people‚ÅÝ‚ÄîDoctor Pyle and Doctor Carter, and the redheaded Watson boy, who‚Äôs grown a moustache and glasses since I saw him last; and fully a dozen of the older nurses‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ And‚ÅÝ‚Äîmy dear‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou should see the Merrick person!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Don‚Äôt frown that way!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ I know you never liked him.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou mean I never knew him.‚Äù

‚ÄúOf course! That was the whole trouble! You never met him but once, on that awful night! Ugh!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ that night!‚Äù

Helen patted her hand.

‚ÄúForget it! We‚Äôll never mention it again!‚Äù

Joyce brightened and resumed her monologue.

‚ÄúWell, as I was saying, all you ever knew about him was that he had a sentimental notion he must study surgery and try to take dear dad‚Äôs place‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ And you thought it a piece of impudence, didn‚Äôt you?‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Listen! He‚Äôs going to come near doing it! Do you know what Bobby Merrick‚Äôs up and done? He‚Äôs invented a thing‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

The taxi scraped against the kerb, and the doorman was reaching for the baggage.

‚ÄúLook! Isn‚Äôt this foxy?‚Äù prattled Joyce, as they entered the lobby. ‚ÄúAll done over!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Let‚Äôs go straight through to luncheon. I‚Äôm starving!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ There‚Äôs a place‚ÅÝ‚Äîby the window‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Umm! Duck! That‚Äôs Christmassy!‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat were you saying about your work at the hospital, when I interrupted you?‚Äù asked Helen, as the waiter moved away, scribbling on his card.

‚ÄúWhat was I saying?‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Oh, yes‚ÅÝ‚Äîabout Bobby! He‚Äôs made an electrical thing that‚Äôs bringing head surgeons here from all over. I don‚Äôt know exactly what it is‚ÅÝ‚Äîsome sort of a charged knife‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ awfully complicated‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ They‚Äôre doing operations with it at Brightwood that have never been done anywhere before‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Something that prevents haemorrhages, or something. Nancy Ashford told me about it; but she couldn‚Äôt explain it very well. I saw it‚ÅÝ‚Äîa big, tall, wooden case full of the most intricate machinery you ever saw‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúGlass things?‚Äù

‚ÄúUmm-humm!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ How did you know?‚Äù

‚ÄúWell‚ÅÝ‚Äîthey always do have a lot of‚ÅÝ‚Äîof things made of glass whenever they use electricity, don‚Äôt they?‚Äù

‚ÄúNaturally‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Have some of that wonderful celery, darling‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Only celery in the world, you know‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ And handsome? My word! Honestly‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI wouldn‚Äôt have known him! And he always was good-looking!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ But the change in him is simply marvellous! I‚Äôm half afraid of him‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Oh‚ÅÝ‚Äîvery professional! Crisp! No nonsense‚ÅÝ‚Äîno‚ÅÝ‚Äîsir-ee! And the nurses are all wild and crazy about him‚ÅÝ‚Äîand he doesn‚Äôt know they‚Äôre existing‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ He‚Äôs doing some of their most important cases out there, now‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Calls me ‚ÄòMrs.¬ÝMasterson!‚Äô Isn‚Äôt that idiotic! Nancy says it‚Äôs because I‚Äôm employed there‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Funny‚ÅÝ‚Äîdon‚Äôt you think?‚Äù

Helen thought it was funny; thought the whole speech funny.

‚ÄúReally‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù Joyce leaned forward and dropped her voice. ‚ÄúIf I had the teeniest right to, I‚Äôd lose my heart‚ÅÝ‚Äîutterly! You know I always was a little bit soft about him!‚Äù

‚ÄúI hope you‚Äôll not be indiscreet, Joyce.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, I‚Äôll take pains not to let him know I think he‚Äôs nice,‚Äù she reassured. ‚ÄúBut‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI don‚Äôt want you feeling unkindly toward him. You really can‚Äôt avoid meeting him, you know.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhy not?‚Äù

‚ÄúWell‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou‚Äôll be out at Brightwood, more or less, now I‚Äôm there‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt see how your work at Brightwood would require my presence.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou‚Äôre going out, aren‚Äôt you?‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Honestly, darling, you‚Äôll just have to be pleasant toward him‚ÅÝ‚Äîfor my sake!‚Äù

‚ÄúFor your sake?‚Äù

Helen experienced a momentary wave of disappointment‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ In her long absence from Joyce, she had idealized her somewhat; but‚ÅÝ‚Äîshe was the same Joyce, whom no blunders could chasten; the same unchanged Joyce who couldn‚Äôt forget anything, or learn anything; hopping gaily out of the frying-pan into the fire.

‚ÄúYes,‚Äù repeated Joyce dramatically, ‚Äúfor my sake!‚Äù

‚ÄúThen,‚Äù said Helen slowly, ‚ÄúI‚Äôm sorry I came back.‚Äù

They ate their duck; but it wasn‚Äôt very good.

That night they saw The Hypotenuse. It was a rollicking comedy employing a small cast and simple materials. A young widow and her contemporary stepdaughter furtively concealed from each other, throughout the first act, that the late Judge Haskins‚Äô junior partner was of larger interest than was demanded by his professional service as their attorney and business counsellor. Act Two developed some delicious situations, adroitly handled. The audience was delighted.

When the curtain had finally settled, after several trips in response to warm applause which brought them all back together, and then reconsidered them in relays, finishing with mamma and Polly, hand in hand, Joyce turned animatedly to offer comment and surprised an abstracted look on Helen‚Äôs face. She, apparently, had not been quite prepared for the sudden onrush of lights.

‚ÄúYou‚Äôre tired, aren‚Äôt you?‚Äù inquired Joyce solicitously.

‚ÄúIt always takes a day or two, I think, to recover from a long journey.‚Äù

‚ÄúAwfully jolly show, don‚Äôt you think? Fancy such a mess! You and I‚ÅÝ‚Äîfor instance! We‚Äôd be honest with each other, at least! I suppose we‚Äôd just cut the deck to see which one was to get him.‚Äù

‚ÄúStuffy in here, isn‚Äôt it?‚Äù said Helen. ‚ÄúLet‚Äôs go out and prowl in the foyer. Want to?‚Äù

Joyce‚Äôs incapacity for understanding the operations of her own mind was spectacularly displayed, early next morning, as she dressed to go to Brightwood, babbling about herself as a ‚Äúwoiking goil.‚Äù

Breakfast had been served in their rooms. Helen, exquisite in a dainty lounging robe, was lingering over her coffee and the morning papers.

‚ÄúI think it‚Äôs simply marvellous,‚Äù enthused Joyce, into the mirror, ‚Äúthat I‚Äôve been able to adjust so quickly to office routine, don‚Äôt you?‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ After all these years of indulging myself, sleeping late, pottering, lazing about! I‚Äôm happier than I ever thought I could be again. I know now I‚Äôll never be contented, any more, without a regular job.‚Äù

‚ÄúGlad you like it,‚Äù said Helen, deep in the theatre advertisements. ‚ÄúWhat do people say about this new musical comedy, Jasmine?‚Äù

‚ÄúVery tuneful, I hear‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Like this hat?‚Äù

‚ÄúCute!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Suppose Mrs.¬ÝAshford might like to come down town, tomorrow, and have dinner with us and see Jasmine?‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, I‚Äôm sure she would love it! I‚Äôll ask her. And, darling‚ÅÝ‚Äîshe and Bobby Merrick are such pals. Wouldn‚Äôt it be nice to ask him, too?‚Äù

‚ÄúNot a bit nice! I don‚Äôt know him! I don‚Äôt want to know him!‚Äù Helen‚Äôs tone was frankly impatient.

‚ÄúWell, you could get acquainted with him. I know him! Couldn‚Äôt you consider my wishes a little?‚Äù Joyce savagely flicked probable dust from her coat.

‚ÄúHand me my pocketbook‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ there‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ on the mantel. Thanks!‚Äù

Helen opened it and unfolded a letter.

‚ÄúOh‚ÅÝ‚Äîit‚Äôs the one I wrote you!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Well, what about it?‚Äù

‚ÄúRead it‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ You will see that you asked me to travel five thousand miles to give you some good advice. Now that I‚Äôve gone to the bother of humouring you, I hope you‚Äôll not resent it if I say that your present state of mind in regard to Doctor Merrick is absurd!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ If you want to be silly about him, don‚Äôt annoy me with it! I won‚Äôt have him thrust down my throat!‚Äù

‚ÄúWell‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhat‚Äôs come over you? I hadn‚Äôt heard that residence in France and Italy made people so squeamish!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ And it seems to me that a person with your admiration for people who do really valorous things‚ÅÝ‚Äîat a lot of cost to themselves‚ÅÝ‚Äîwould take a little human interest in Bobby Merrick, slaving himself almost to death, when, if he wanted to, he might be lounging on a yacht somewhere in the Mediterranean!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ I heard one of the nurses say he had come into a million dollars‚Äô worth of Axion Motors when he was twenty-five, and is due for another million when he is thirty! I tell you he deserves some credit!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Goodbye‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Don‚Äôt be peeved!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ See you about five-thirty.‚Äù

Helen rose, after the door had slammed, and stood looking down upon the street‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Axion Motors!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ A million dollars‚Äô worth of Axion Motors!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Axion!
